facts_disclosed: Developed patellofemoral syndrome (runner's knee) and had to stop running
Starting to love cycling for its low impact

turns.content: So remember how I was training for the marathon? Yeah. That is NOT happening anymore haha. I have some frustrating news.
Oh no — what happened? You were so committed to that training plan.

My knee happened. I'd been dealing with anterior knee pain for a while and kept ignoring it because — and this is the embarrassing part — I'm a PHYSICAL THERAPIST. I literally treat this stuff every day but when it's your own body you become the worst patient. Told myself it was just overuse, foam roll it, it'll be fine. Spoiler: not fine.
That's actually really common among healthcare professionals — the "I'll just push through it" mindset. What did the imaging end up showing?

Patellofemoral syndrome. Classic runner's knee! Ai meu Deus, the irony is KILLING me. My colleague Sara literally laughed in my face when I told her. A physical therapist with runner's knee — gente, it's like a dentist with cavities! I know every single protocol, every exercise, every progression, and I still did this to myself by being stubborn.

The irony is painful, but you're far from the only clinician who's been there. Knowing the protocols doesn't make you immune to the injury — especially with the training volume you were doing. What does this mean for running long term?

The honest truth? Running five times a week, the high mileage, the pavement pounding — that chapter is probably over. My cartilage is not happy and at 41 I need to be realistic about what my joints can handle long-term. So I made the hard decision to stop running completely.
That's a really significant decision. Running wasn't just exercise for you — it sounded like it was tied to your daily routine, your mental health, your identity. How are you processing that?

I cried, querida. Like actually cried. Running was my therapy, my meditation, my mornings at 5am before the heat hit. The streets are empty, the city is quiet, it was MY time. Losing that felt like losing a part of myself. And I know that sounds dramatic for 'just exercise' but anyone who runs knows — it's never just exercise.
It doesn't sound dramatic at all — grieving a movement practice that was central to your identity and your coping is completely valid. Have you started thinking about what might fill that space?

Yes!! And okay here's the silver lining — I switched to cycling and I'm starting to genuinely LOVE it! Got a proper road bike, joined a cycling group that does Al Qudra on Friday mornings. Zero impact on my knee, I still get the cardio and the outdoor feeling. The wind, the desert, the sunrise — nossa, it's honestly beautiful.

Cycling is a great pivot biomechanically — it preserves the cardiovascular benefits and the outdoor experience while keeping the knee in a controlled, low-impact range of motion. How does the knee actually feel when you ride?

Zero pain! The knee stays in a controlled range, no impact loading, no eccentric stress on the patella. It's the perfect swap and I say that as someone who prescribes exactly this transition to my patients all the time haha. One of the guys in the group is an ex-triathlete and he's been coaching me on cadence and positioning. I'm getting faster every week!

Having someone coach you on technique from the start is smart — cycling mechanics make a big difference in both performance and joint protection. Are you looking at this recreationally or are you already eyeing races?

You know me, meu bem — I can't do anything halfway haha! I already have a training plan and I'm looking at amateur races. The group is competitive so it pushes me. I went from 'I guess I'll try cycling' to 'I need a power meter and aero wheels' in about three weeks. My bank account is not happy but my knee is!
That escalation sounds very on-brand. Has going through this yourself changed how you relate to patients who have to give up their sport?

SO much! Now when a patient sits in front of me devastated because they can't do their sport anymore, I really FEEL it. Before I could empathize intellectually but now I understand the grief on a visceral level. It's like losing a friend. But I also understand now that the body adapts, you find new things, and sometimes what you're forced into ends up being exactly what you needed.

There's actually research showing that clinicians who've experienced injury themselves tend to be more effective at guiding patients through the psychological side of recovery — which is often the hardest part. Your patients are lucky to have someone who truly gets it now.

I believe that! The physical rehab I can do in my sleep, but the emotional part — the identity crisis, the fear, the frustration — that's where people really struggle. My mae saw my cycling photos and said 'Bruna, you look ridiculous in that helmet' — mothers are so supportive haha!
Your mom's commentary never disappoints. But seriously — you've handled this transition with a lot of resilience. Going from grief to genuinely embracing something new this quickly says a lot.

Thank you! I tell my patients all the time — movement is movement, the form doesn't matter as long as you're moving. Now I finally had to take my own advice. And honestly? Cycling might be the best thing that ever happened to my knees AND my soul. Plus I look incredible in cycling shorts, so there's that haha!
If the biomechanics check out and the fashion works too, I'd say this transition is a complete success. Keep enjoying it, and keep being kind to those knees.
